narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your strong LSAT score of 42, which stands out well above the typical 36.7 average, along with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.10 and an upper-middle family income background, while planning to attend full-time like most applicants. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours from past students, estimated a 60.6% chance you'll fail the bar—high enough to trigger an automatic rejection to protect the school's outcomes. This decision hinged mostly on your academic deciles from earlier years in school: your Year 3 decile of 2 was the biggest factor pushing the prediction toward failure, as it signals performance in the lower range compared to peers who typically land around the middle of their class. Your Year 1 decile of 2 played a supporting role in the same direction, reinforcing concerns about consistency in your grades relative to the average of about 5.7. On a brighter note, your standout LSAT score strongly pulled the prediction the other way, toward expecting you to pass, nearly offsetting those earlier academic signals. Overall, these three elements—Year 3 decile first, LSAT second, and Year 1 decile third—dominated the model's thinking for your specific profile. Compared to typical students who pass, your early deciles are notably lower, which weighed more heavily here than your other strengths. While this stings, especially with your impressive LSAT as a Hispanic applicant often facing extra hurdles, it doesn't define your potential. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your recent academic deciles, as schools value demonstrated improvement. You might also target programs with holistic reviews that weigh test scores like your LSAT more prominently. Keep building your story—many overcome similar predictions through persistence.
